exemplar.title: How do different ratios of Ethanol and 2-Propanol (2%, 20%, 40%, 60% and 80% 2-Propanol) in an Ethanol-2-Propanol mixture affect the enthalpy of combustion as determined by calorimetry?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do different ratios of Ethanol and 2-Propanol (2%, 20%, 40%, 60% and 80% 2-Propanol) in an Ethanol-2-Propanol mixture affect the enthalpy of combustion as determined by calorimetry?

strengths: Large data sets are well organized in labelled tables and graphs, making the overall trends easy to follow.
Uncertainties for Q, n and ÎHâ are stated and combined to give percentage uncertainties.
Data processing (calculations of ÎT, Q, moles and ÎHâ) is applied consistently across trials.
weaknesses: Extrapolation method is flawed (only heating-phase data used) and no regression statistics (rÂ², standard error) are reported.
Full uncertainty propagation (including calorimeter heat capacity and extrapolation uncertainty) is omitted.
Inconsistencies in sign conventions for ÎHâ and typos in table headers reduce precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data is clear but lacks full precision; uncertainties are considered but with significant omissions; processing shows some inaccuracies. These factors place the work above the rudimentary stage but below excellent precision. A score of 4/6 captures solid effort with room for improvement in regression details and uncertainty propagation.

strengths: Research question is clearly defined with specific concentrations (2â80 % v/v) and linked to realâworld bioethanol impurity levels, demonstrating strong context and relevance.
Background sections thoroughly explain the industrial context, chemical rationale and expected thermodynamic consequences.
Method description (step-by-step procedure, materials list and variable tables) allows replication with only minor ambiguities.
weaknesses: Omission of calorimeter heat-capacity discussion limits methodological rigor.
Typographical errors in units (e.g. â2 cmÂ²â instead of cmÂ³) introduce ambiguity.
Materials list and variables table lack some unit specifications and consistency, which could hinder full reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research question is situated within a precise, industry-relevant context and the student provides a generally reproducible method. Minor lapses (unit typos, incomplete discussion of heat capacity, some table formatting issues) prevent a perfect score but performance clearly exceeds the middle band. A holistic score of 5/6 reflects strong contextualization and sufficient methodological detail with only moderate ambiguities.

strengths: Conclusion restates the observed positive correlation and references both mean ÎHâ values and the rÂ² statistic.
Acknowledges both statistical uncertainty (Â±0.9â1.5 %) and systematic bias (up to 81 % error), demonstrating critical reflection.
Compares experimental ÎHâ values to literature by calculating percentage errors and invoking bond-energy arguments.
weaknesses: Discussion lacks quantitative exploration of deviations at specific concentrations (e.g. 60 %).
Graph and cross-reference numbering errors (e.g. incorrect âGraph 1â citation) reduce clarity.
Justification remains largely qualitative and does not fully integrate numerical residuals or outlier analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is relevant and generally supported by the analysis, with a reasonable comparison to scientific context. It goes beyond a simple restatement by addressing uncertainty and literature values, though it stops short of fully quantitative justification. A score of 5/6 reflects strong integration of results and context with only moderate weaknesses in numerical depth and presentation accuracy.

strengths: Evaluation lists specific methodological weaknesses (e.g. heat loss via draught, incomplete combustion) with clear impact descriptions.
Proposes realistic, chemistry-based improvements (draught shield, oxygen feed, probe spacer) directly linked to the identified errors.
Explains the relative magnitude and direction of each errorâs impact on ÎHâ, distinguishing systematic vs random effects.
weaknesses: Minor repetition in the wording of some improvement suggestions.
Could further consider secondary factors (e.g. calorimeter insulation, cost/resource constraints) for a more holistic evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates comprehensive reflection on the experimental limitations, with well-justified, relevant improvements and clear impact analysis. It meets all descriptors in the top band for both strands, meriting the maximum score of 6/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a focused, quantitative research question that is explicitly linked to real-world bioethanol production and the known presence of 2-propanol impurities. Background sections (2.1-2.4) clearly explain the industrial context, chemical rationale and expected thermodynamic consequences. The specificity of concentrations (2â80 % v/v) and the justification anchored in impurity levels encountered in industry represent an appropriate and well-delineated context. Minor stylistic lapses do not diminish the relevance or specificity, so performance aligns with the 5-6 band.

Calculations of ÎT, Q, moles and ÎHc are shown and consistently applied across trials, and means are plotted. However, the extrapolation method is flawed (simple linear regression of cooling phase), accepted values are mis-handled and sign conventions occasionally inconsistent. These issues constitute âsignificant inaccuraciesâ yet the processing is more than rudimentary, justifying placement in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

Large data sets are provided and organised in labelled tables and graphs, but table 3 is corrupted and headers are misaligned; figure captions are sometimes generic. Processed-data tables include uncertainties but units are occasionally omitted. Overall communication is clear enough to follow (clarity) yet lacks full precision and polish, so the work satisfies âeither clear or preciseâ rather than both.

For each weakness, the student proposes a realistic, chemistry-based improvement and explains how it would mitigate the specific error (e.g., draught shield to reduce heat loss, oxygen feed to ensure complete combustion, spacer for probe). Explanations link directly to data accuracy and reliability, satisfying the criterion for âexplains relevant realistic improvementsâ. Minor repetition keeps the score at 5 rather than 6.

Instrument uncertainties are stated and combined to obtain percentage uncertainties for Q, n and ÎHc. Nevertheless, calorimeter heat capacity, extrapolation uncertainty and propagated uncertainty in the mean values are omitted, and some rounding errors appear. The evidence therefore shows consideration but with significant omissions, fitting the 3-4 band at the lower end.

The student compares results to literature ÎHc values by calculating percentage errors and invoking bond-energy arguments. While relevant, the discussion is limited and does not thoroughly explore discrepancies with published combustion enthalpies or calibrationâfree calorimetry studies. The comparison is therefore present and somewhat relevant, but not deeply justified, aligning with the middle band.

Five specific weaknesses are listed, each accompanied by an explanation of how the error biased ÎHc (direction and magnitude) and whether it was systematic or random. The relative impact (e.g., major vs minor, influence on Q or Îm) is explicitly stated, matching the descriptor of explaining the relative impact. Minor wording issues prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Methodological choices (choice of range, five trials, insulation steps, watch glasses, LabQuest sampling rate, variable control table) are described and generally linked to obtaining sufficient and relevant data. However, some explanations remain surface level (e.g., no quantitative rationale for five-minute burn or 100 mL water, no discussion of calorimeter heat capacity), so the considerations cannot be viewed as âfully explainedâ. The description nonetheless goes beyond merely stating basics, placing it solidly in the 3-4 band.

The step-by-step method, materials list and variable tables would allow another student to replicate the work, but several ambiguities remain: units are occasionally mistyped ("2 cmÂ²" instead of cmÂ³), offsets in tables, omission of burner height, and no instruction on how to position a draught shield later mentioned. These gaps introduce moderate uncertainty yet do not prevent reproduction altogether, matching the middle of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates the observed positive correlation and references mean ÎHc values and the rÂ² statistic. It acknowledges large percentage errors and reliability concerns, but the justification remains qualitative and does not fully integrate the numerical analysis (e.g., no quantitative discussion of trend deviation at 60 %). Hence the conclusion is âdescribed but not fully consistentâ, meriting the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly defined with specific concentrations (2â80 %) and linked to real-world bioethanol impurity levels, demonstrating strong context and relevance.
The background on ethanol and 2-propanol structure is thorough, but referencing more recent thermochemical data could strengthen the justification of expected ÎHc differences.

While the method development explains choice of range and duration, it omits discussion of the calorimeterâs heat capacity. Including a quantitative heatâcapacity consideration would improve methodological rigor.
The unit for 2-propanol volume is mistyped as â2 cmÂ²â instead of cmÂ³, introducing ambiguity. Correcting this to 2 cmÂ³ (or a volume measure) is essential for reproducibility.
The variables table omits units for the intervals/method description. Including explicit units (e.g., cmÂ³ for fuel, seconds for time) would enhance clarity and precision.

Controlledâvariable table conflates header formatting with data cells and repeats ethanol/2-propanol concentration entries. Refining this table structure would improve readability and avoid duplication.

The extrapolation graph uses only the heating phase data and omits the coolingâphase fit, which can misrepresent peak temperature. Applying regression to the cooling tail improves theoretical peak estimation.

The reported trendline $$f(x)=-0.0425x+59.7$$ lacks reporting of the regression coefficient and standard error. Including $$r^2$$ and uncertainty in slope/intercept would strengthen data communication.

Uncertainty propagation is not shown for the extrapolated temperature or subsequent $$Q$$ calculations. Demonstrating full propagation through to $$
9;\Delta H_c$$ would improve precision assessment.
The computed $$\Delta H_c = 232\,\mathrm{kJ/mol}$$ changes sign manually rather than using a consistent sign convention in the formula. Apply a clear sign rule in your derivation.
Heatâcapacity of the calorimeter and residual heat in burner are not included in the uncertainty budget. Including these systematic uncertainties would yield more accurate error estimates.
Table 8 includes a header âE. Final Water Temp.Â¹Â¹â which seems inconsistent; ensure caption footnote labels match figure/table references and correct typos.
In the processed data for 40% (Table 10), the mean Mr increases unexpectedly; verify your combined molar mass calculation for each concentration.
Percentage uncertainties in mean ÎHc are not propagated to the average values (Table 13). Including propagation when computing Â±% on means would improve precision.
The conclusion cites an rÂ² of 0.756 but incorrectly references âGraph 1â instead of the final trendline. Ensure cross-references and graph numbering are accurate.
The narrative notes slight deviation between 40% and 80% mixtures but lacks quantitative discussion of these outliers. Including numerical residuals or percent deviation would deepen analysis.
Acknowledging both statistical uncertainty (Â± 0.9â1.5 %) and systematic bias (81% error) shows critical reflection on result reliability, supporting the conclusion.
The evaluation table lists specific methodological weaknesses with clear impact descriptions and targeted improvements, demonstrating thorough reflection on experimental limitations.
The suggested extension to study ethyl acetate is relevant to bioethanol impurity research; consider outlining a brief method adaptation to demonstrate feasibility.
The background discussion on 2-propanol assumes bondâlength sum correlates directly with ÎHc; note that bond energies, not lengths, determine enthalpy changes and discuss this nuance.
The ethanol structure section could incorporate enthalpy of formation tables to justify the cited â1367 kJ/mol. Explicit thermochemical data would enhance context.
Ensure all footnotes consistently follow a single citation format. Inconsistent styles were noted between IEA and PubChem entries, which can confuse readers.
The materials list omits units for some items (e.g., â1Ã Lighterâ is clear but â1Ã Calorimeter Standâ could specify type or model). Adding detail improves reproducibility.
